food_subgroup: Game meat dishes — stir-fried venison with aromatics

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general deer meat food product class (FOODON_03311737). No specific entry exists for ginger-and-onion prepared venison dish; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers venison/game meat as a raw commodity. No prepared stir-fried venison dish code exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 identifies venison as a substance. No specific prepared dish code exists; closest_parent applied.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Ginger and Onion Deer Meat is approximately 250 g, comprising sliced venison stir-fried with ginger, onion, and aromatics, served as a shared main dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Venison, raw (Deer meat, raw) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 17162; adapted with cooking-loss factor and condiment additions for stir-fried preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw venison values from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 17162 / FDC 174299) were adapted for a stir-fried preparation. A standard stir-fry cooking-loss factor of approximately 20% moisture loss was applied to protein and fat concentrations. Sodium was increased to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce additions typical of Chinese stir-fry. Carbohydrate and sugar were adjusted upward to reflect oyster sauce and trace cornstarch marinade. Values represent a best-estimate composite for the finished dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on cooked lean meat (GI ~0) with minor sauce carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure lean meat has a GI of effectively 0 as it contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates. The stir-fried preparation includes oyster sauce and trace cornstarch, contributing approximately 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g. GI is estimated at 20 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed meat-and-sauce dishes with low carbohydrate content, consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed GI tables. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (20 × 10.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium — roughly half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~20) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g); high protein supports satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ginger and Onion Deer Meat is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments, as its low GI and high lean protein content support glycaemic control and prolonged satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium; venison is leaner than most red meats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Venison is a lean red meat with lower saturated fat than beef or pork, making it a preferable red meat choice for cardiovascular health; however, the sodium load from sauce ingredients warrants moderation in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger and Onion Deer Meat (姜葱鹿肉, Jiang Cong Lu Rou) is a Chinese-style stir-fried dish featuring thinly sliced venison (deer meat) cooked in a wok with fresh ginger, onion, spring onion, garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, finished with sesame oil. The dish is a specialty found in select Chinese seafood and game-meat restaurants in Singapore, such as Riverfront Seafood at Boat Quay. Venison is prized in Chinese culinary tradition for its lean, dense texture and mild gamey flavour, which is balanced by the pungent warmth of ginger and the sweetness of onion. The stir-fry technique produces tender, slightly caramelised meat with a savoury, aromatic sauce. Nutritionally, venison is one of the leanest red meats available, providing approximately 22.5 g protein and only 4.8 g fat per 100 g, with a favourable iron content (~3.2 mg per 100 g) and high B-vitamin levels including B12 (~2.1 mcg). The glycemic index is low (~20), making it appropriate for blood sugar management. The primary clinical concern is sodium from the sauce base (~480 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for non-vegetarian diets. It is typically served as a shared main course alongside steamed jasmine rice in a Chinese restaurant setting.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw venison data (FDC 174299) with stir-fry cooking adjustments and sauce additions. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (venison) has strong USDA data support and the adaptation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw venison (FDC 174299) with a ~20% moisture-loss cooking factor applied and sodium increased to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce additions. This is an estimated composite for the finished stir-fried dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy venison entry and no reliable secondary source was available for this specific preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in lean game meat per USDA data. 4. GI is estimated at 20 (low) based on analogous lean meat-and-sauce dish category from Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. The input cuisine_tags listed 'Seafood Dish', 'Crab', and 'Lobster' — these are restaurant-level tags for Riverfront Seafood and do not describe this dish; they have been disregarded. 6. Halal status is marked false as deer meat preparation and sauce sourcing cannot be confirmed halal without restaurant certification. 7. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes use closest_parent match type as no exact prepared dish codes exist in these ontologies for stir-fried venison.

Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value with explicit justification in notes.
Set GI = 20 (low) with peer-reviewed analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 AJCN.
